stimuli.description: Inter-block attention check prompt rendered by jspsych-attention-check-rdoc plugin (e.g., 'Press the q key' or 'Press the key for the first letter of the English alphabet'). Detected before regular stimuli so the bot routes it to attention-check handling.
Color word whose meaning matches its ink color (e.g., 'red' in red ink). Detected by reading the .stroop-stim element's textContent and computed inline color, then comparing word == color.

detection.selector: (() => { const el = document.querySelector('#jspsych-html-keyboard-response-stimulus .stroop-stim') || document.querySelector('.stroop-stim'); if (!el) return false; const word = (el.textContent || '').trim().toLowerCase(); const c = (el.style.color || '').replace(/\s/g, '').toLowerCase(); let color = null; if (c.indexOf('255,112,112') !== -1 || c === '#ff7070') color = 'red'; else if (c.indexOf('112,112,255') !== -1 || c === '#7070ff') color = 'blue'; else if (c.indexOf('112,255,112') !== -1 || c === '#70ff70') color = 'green'; return color !== null && word === color; })()

response.response_key_js: (() => { if (typeof window.correctResponse !== 'undefined') return window.correctResponse; try { if (typeof jsPsych !== 'undefined' && jsPsych.getCurrentTrial) { const t = jsPsych.getCurrentTrial(); if (t) { let d = t.data; if (typeof d === 'function') d = d(); if (d && d.correct_response) return d.correct_response; } } } catch(e) {} const el = document.querySelector('#jspsych-html-keyboard-response-stimulus .stroop-stim') || document.querySelector('.stroop-stim'); if (!el) return null; const c = (el.style.color || '').replace(/\s/g, '').toLowerCase(); let colorName = null; if (c.indexOf('255,112,112') !== -1 || c === '#ff7070') colorName = 'red'; else if (c.indexOf('112,112,255') !== -1 || c === '#7070ff') colorName = 'blue'; else if (c.indexOf('112,255,112') !== -1 || c === '#70ff70') colorName = 'green'; if (!colorName) return null; const g = (window.efVars && typeof window.efVars.group_index === 'number') ? window.efVars.group_index : 1; let map; if (g <= 2) map = {red: ',', blue: '.', green: '/'}; else if (g <= 5) map = {red: ',', blue: '/', green: '.'}; else if (g <= 8) map = {red: '/', blue: ',', green: '.'}; else if (g <= 11) map = {red: '/', blue: '.', green: ','}; else map = {red: '.', blue: '/', green: ','}; return map[colorName] || null; })()

stimuli.description: Color word whose meaning mismatches its ink color (e.g., 'red' in green ink). Same detection logic as congruent but with word != color.

detection.selector: (() => { const el = document.querySelector('#jspsych-html-keyboard-response-stimulus .stroop-stim') || document.querySelector('.stroop-stim'); if (!el) return false; const word = (el.textContent || '').trim().toLowerCase(); const c = (el.style.color || '').replace(/\s/g, '').toLowerCase(); let color = null; if (c.indexOf('255,112,112') !== -1 || c === '#ff7070') color = 'red'; else if (c.indexOf('112,112,255') !== -1 || c === '#7070ff') color = 'blue'; else if (c.indexOf('112,255,112') !== -1 || c === '#70ff70') color = 'green'; return color !== null && ['red','blue','green'].indexOf(word) !== -1 && word !== color; })()

response.response_key_js: (() => { if (typeof window.correctResponse !== 'undefined') return window.correctResponse; try { if (typeof jsPsych !== 'undefined' && jsPsych.getCurrentTrial) { const t = jsPsych.getCurrentTrial(); if (t) { let d = t.data; if (typeof d === 'function') d = d(); if (d && d.correct_response) return d.correct_response; } } } catch(e) {} const el = document.querySelector('#jspsych-html-keyboard-response-stimulus .stroop-stim') || document.querySelector('.stroop-stim'); if (!el) return null; const c = (el.style.color || '').replace(/\s/g, '').toLowerCase(); let colorName = null; if (c.indexOf('255,112,112') !== -1 || c === '#ff7070') colorName = 'red'; else if (c.indexOf('112,112,255') !== -1 || c === '#7070ff') colorName = 'blue'; else if (c.indexOf('112,255,112') !== -1 || c === '#70ff70') colorName = 'green'; if (!colorName) return null; const g = (window.efVars && typeof window.efVars.group_index === 'number') ? window.efVars.group_index : 1; let map; if (g <= 2) map = {red: ',', blue: '.', green: '/'}; else if (g <= 5) map = {red: ',', blue: '/', green: '.'}; else if (g <= 8) map = {red: '/', blue: ',', green: '.'}; else if (g <= 11) map = {red: '/', blue: '.', green: ','}; else map = {red: '.', blue: '/', green: ','}; return map[colorName] || null; })()

phase_detection.test: (() => { try { if (typeof jsPsych === 'undefined') return false; const trials = jsPsych.data.get().filter({trial_id: 'test_trial'}).count(); return trials > 0 && document.querySelector('.stroop-stim') !== null; } catch(e) { return false; } })()
phase_detection.practice: (() => { try { if (typeof jsPsych === 'undefined') return false; const trials = jsPsych.data.get().filter({trial_id: 'practice_trial'}).count(); return trials > 0 && document.querySelector('.stroop-stim') !== null; } catch(e) { return false; } })()
phase_detection.feedback: (() => { if (document.querySelector('#finished_message')) return false; if (document.querySelector('.stroop-stim')) return false; if (document.querySelector('.fixation')) return false; const el = document.querySelector('#jspsych-content') || document.body; if (!el) return false; const t = (el.textContent || ''); return /Press.*<i>?\s*enter|Press\s+enter|press\s+<i>?\s*enter/i.test(t); })()

timing.trial_context_js: (() => { const el = document.querySelector('.stroop-stim'); if (!el) return ''; return (el.textContent || '').trim() + '/' + (el.style.color || ''); })()

attention_check.response_js: (() => { const el = document.querySelector('#jspsych-attention-check-rdoc-stimulus'); if (!el) return null; const text = (el.textContent || '').toLowerCase(); const m = text.match(/press the ([a-z]) key/); if (m) return m[1]; if (text.indexOf('third to last') !== -1) return 'x'; if (text.indexOf('second to last') !== -1) return 'y'; if (text.indexOf('last letter') !== -1) return 'z'; if (text.indexOf('first letter') !== -1) return 'a'; if (text.indexOf('second letter') !== -1) return 'b'; if (text.indexOf('third letter') !== -1) return 'c'; return null; })()

counterbalancing.rationale: Per-participant color→key assignment derived from window.efVars.group_index in the source code's getKeyMappingForTask function. Five distinct mappings cover indices 0–14. The response_key_js fallback chain reproduces this mapping when window.correctResponse and the per-trial jsPsych data are unavailable.

citations.authors: Heathcote, A., Popiel, S. J., & Mewhort, D. J. K.
citations.title: Analysis of response time distributions: An example using the Stroop task
citations.quote: Ex-Gaussian fits to color-word Stroop in young adults yielded mu estimates for congruent trials in the range of approximately 470–530 ms across replications.

citations.authors: Spieler, D. H., Balota, D. A., & Faust, M. E.
citations.title: Stroop performance in healthy younger and older adults and in individuals with dementia of the Alzheimer's type
citations.quote: Young adult congruent-condition ex-Gaussian mu was on the order of 490 ms, with sigma and tau components separately estimated.

citations.authors: MacLeod, C. M.
citations.title: Half a century of research on the Stroop effect: An integrative review
citations.quote: Mean color-naming RTs on congruent trials in healthy young adults cluster around 600 ms, with the Gaussian component sitting roughly 80–100 ms below the mean once an exponential tail is partialled out.

citations.authors: Heathcote, A., Popiel, S. J., & Mewhort, D. J. K.
citations.title: Analysis of response time distributions: An example using the Stroop task
citations.quote: The Gaussian SD (sigma) of the ex-Gaussian fit to congruent Stroop trials ranged across subjects roughly between 35 and 70 ms.

citations.authors: Spieler, D. H., Balota, D. A., & Faust, M. E.
citations.title: Stroop performance in healthy younger and older adults and in individuals with dementia of the Alzheimer's type
citations.quote: Sigma for congruent Stroop trials in young adults averaged approximately 50–60 ms.

citations.authors: Heathcote, A., Popiel, S. J., & Mewhort, D. J. K.
citations.title: Analysis of response time distributions: An example using the Stroop task
citations.quote: Tau (exponential component) for congruent Stroop trials in young adults fell typically in the 75–130 ms range across subjects and replications.

citations.authors: Spieler, D. H., Balota, D. A., & Faust, M. E.
citations.title: Stroop performance in healthy younger and older adults and in individuals with dementia of the Alzheimer's type
citations.quote: Congruent-condition tau averaged ~90 ms in young adults, with individual estimates spanning roughly 60 to 140 ms.

congruent.rationale: Color-word Stroop congruent trials in healthy young adults are well-fit by an ex-Gaussian with mean RT ~600-650 ms; the Gaussian component (mu) sits around 500-540 ms with moderate sigma (~50-60 ms) and a right tail tau of ~80-100 ms typical of choice-RT distributions.

citations.authors: Heathcote, A., Popiel, S. J., & Mewhort, D. J. K.
citations.title: Analysis of response time distributions: An example using the Stroop task
citations.quote: Mu for incongruent trials sat ~40–60 ms above the congruent mu in young adults, falling in the approximate range of 510–590 ms.

citations.authors: Spieler, D. H., Balota, D. A., & Faust, M. E.
citations.title: Stroop performance in healthy younger and older adults and in individuals with dementia of the Alzheimer's type
citations.quote: Young-adult incongruent mu averaged approximately 525 ms in the ex-Gaussian decomposition.

citations.authors: MacLeod, C. M.
citations.title: Half a century of research on the Stroop effect: An integrative review
citations.quote: The Stroop interference effect on mean RT is typically 50–100 ms in healthy young adults under standard color-word conditions.

citations.authors: Heathcote, A., Popiel, S. J., & Mewhort, D. J. K.
citations.title: Analysis of response time distributions: An example using the Stroop task
citations.quote: Sigma estimates for incongruent trials were comparable to or modestly larger than congruent, in the range 40–80 ms.

citations.authors: Spieler, D. H., Balota, D. A., & Faust, M. E.
citations.title: Stroop performance in healthy younger and older adults and in individuals with dementia of the Alzheimer's type
citations.quote: Incongruent sigma averaged ~55–65 ms in young adults.

citations.authors: Heathcote, A., Popiel, S. J., & Mewhort, D. J. K.
citations.title: Analysis of response time distributions: An example using the Stroop task
citations.quote: Tau (right-tail exponential) inflated more than mu for incongruent relative to congruent trials, indicating that Stroop interference loads disproportionately onto slow responses; incongruent tau typically 100–160 ms.

citations.authors: Spieler, D. H., Balota, D. A., & Faust, M. E.
citations.title: Stroop performance in healthy younger and older adults and in individuals with dementia of the Alzheimer's type
citations.quote: Incongruent tau averaged ~115 ms in young adults, ~25 ms above the congruent estimate, consistent with conflict-induced inflation of the slow tail.

incongruent.rationale: Incongruent trials show a Stroop interference effect of ~50-100 ms on mean RT, expressed in ex-Gaussian fits as a small upward shift in mu (~40-50 ms) and a larger inflation of the right tail tau (~25-35 ms above congruent), consistent with conflict tasks where interference accumulates in slow responses rather than uniformly shifting the distribution.

citations.authors: Wagenmakers, E.-J., Farrell, S., & Ratcliff, R.
citations.title: Estimation and interpretation of 1/fα noise in human cognition
citations.quote: Lag-1 autocorrelations in serial RT series typically fall in the range ~0.10–0.30 once condition variance is removed, reflecting slow drifts that are not captured by trial-level predictors.

citations.authors: Gilden, D. L.
citations.title: Cognitive emissions of 1/f noise
citations.quote: Positive serial dependence at lag 1 is consistently observed in choice-RT residuals, with phi commonly between 0.15 and 0.25.

citations.authors: Gilden, D. L.
citations.title: Fluctuations in the time required for elementary decisions
citations.quote: Successive trials' RTs in simple and choice tasks show modest positive lag-1 dependence (~0.15–0.20) after removing trend and condition effects.

autocorrelation.rationale: Trial-to-trial RTs in speeded choice tasks show modest positive lag-1 autocorrelation (typical phi ~0.15-0.25), reflecting slow drifts in arousal and motor readiness that are unrelated to condition.

citations.authors: Boksem, M. A. S., Meijman, T. F., & Lorist, M. M.
citations.title: Effects of mental fatigue on attention: An ERP study
citations.quote: RT increased gradually across the 3-hour testing session in a sustained-attention task, equivalent to roughly 0.1–0.3 ms per trial of monotonic time-on-task drift.

citations.authors: Lim, J., & Dinges, D. F.
citations.title: Sleep deprivation and vigilant attention
citations.quote: Time-on-task increases mean RT by tens of milliseconds over a 10–20 minute block, with per-trial increments on the order of 0.1–0.4 ms in cognitive tasks of moderate difficulty.

fatigue_drift.rationale: Long Stroop sessions show a small linear RT increase across trials (on the order of 0.1-0.3 ms/trial) attributable to time-on-task fatigue, separable from practice gains in early blocks.

citations.authors: Bertelson, P.
citations.title: Sequential redundancy and speed in a serial two-choice responding task
citations.quote: Two-choice serial RT was reliably faster (~10–20 ms) when both the stimulus and response repeated relative to alternation.

citations.authors: Soetens, E., Boer, L. C., & Hueting, J. E.
citations.title: Expectancy or automatic facilitation? Separating sequential effects in two-choice reaction time
citations.quote: At short RSIs, repetition produces ~5–15 ms automatic facilitation in two-choice RT, attributable to residual activation of the prior response.

citations.authors: Soetens, E., Boer, L. C., & Hueting, J. E.
citations.title: Expectancy or automatic facilitation? Separating sequential effects in two-choice reaction time
citations.quote: Alternations (switches) produced a small RT cost of ~3–10 ms relative to a neutral baseline at short RSIs.

citations.authors: Bertelson, P.
citations.title: Sequential redundancy and speed in a serial two-choice responding task
citations.quote: Stimulus-response alternations slowed two-choice RT by approximately 5–10 ms compared to repetitions.

condition_repetition.rationale: Choice-RT tasks show small repetition benefits when the same response/condition repeats (~5-15 ms facilitation) and small switch costs (~3-8 ms) when it alternates, independent of condition congruency.

citations.authors: Gilden, D. L.
citations.title: Cognitive emissions of 1/f noise
citations.quote: Residual trial-to-trial RT fluctuation in choice tasks has SD on the order of 15–25 ms once trend and condition variance are removed.

citations.authors: Gilden, D. L.
citations.title: Fluctuations in the time required for elementary decisions
citations.quote: Residual RT noise in serial choice tasks shows SDs in the range of approximately 10–30 ms.

citations.authors: Gilden, D. L.
citations.title: Cognitive emissions of 1/f noise
citations.quote: Spectral analyses of trial-to-trial RT series consistently estimate the 1/f exponent alpha in the range 0.8–1.1, near the canonical value of 1.0.

citations.authors: Wagenmakers, E.-J., Farrell, S., & Ratcliff, R.
citations.title: Estimation and interpretation of 1/fα noise in human cognition
citations.quote: Across replications, the estimated 1/fα exponent for choice-RT residuals clusters around alpha ≈ 0.8–1.1.

pink_noise.rationale: RT time-series in cognitive tasks consistently display 1/f (pink) noise with spectral exponent near 1.0 and trial-to-trial SD of ~15-25 ms once condition and trend variance are removed, reflecting endogenous fluctuations in cognitive control.

citations.authors: Gratton, G., Coles, M. G. H., & Donchin, E.
citations.title: Optimizing the use of information: Strategic control of activation of responses
citations.quote: The interference effect on incongruent trials was reduced by approximately 20–30 ms when the preceding trial was also incongruent (iI) compared to when it was congruent (cI), and a smaller mirroring pattern appeared on congruent trials.

citations.authors: Egner, T., & Hirsch, J.
citations.title: The neural correlates and functional integration of cognitive control in a Stroop task
citations.quote: Stroop interference was reduced by ~15–25 ms following an incongruent trial, replicating the Gratton conflict-adaptation pattern.

citations.authors: Egner, T.
citations.title: Congruency sequence effects and cognitive control
citations.quote: Across conflict-task replications, lag-1 sequence modulation produces ~15–30 ms reduction of interference following an incongruent trial, with a small (~3–8 ms) facilitation on congruent-after-congruent trials.

citations.authors: Schmidt, J. R., & Weissman, D. H.
citations.title: Congruency sequence effects without feature integration or contingency learning confounds
citations.quote: Trials following an error were excluded from the sequence-modulation analysis to avoid contamination by post-error slowing and orienting effects.

citations.authors: Notebaert, W., Houtman, F., Van Opstal, F., Gevers, W., Fias, W., & Verguts, T.
citations.title: Post-error slowing: An orienting account
citations.quote: Post-error trials reflect an orienting response that confounds cognitive-control sequence analyses; standard practice is to discard trials immediately following errors.

lag1_pair_modulation.rationale: Conflict-task literature documents a robust lag-1 pair modulation in which interference on incongruent trials is reduced by ~15-30 ms when preceded by an incongruent trial relative to a congruent prior, with a small mirroring cost on congruent-after-incongruent and slight facilitation on congruent-after-congruent. Modulation is typically suppressed when the prior trial was an error.

citations.authors: Rabbitt, P. M. A.
citations.title: Errors and error correction in choice-response tasks
citations.quote: The trial immediately following an error was reliably slower than baseline trials, with slowing on the order of 30–80 ms.

citations.authors: Dutilh, G., van Ravenzwaaij, D., Nieuwenhuis, S., van der Maas, H. L. J., Forstmann, B. U., & Wagenmakers, E.-J.
citations.title: How to measure post-error slowing: A confound and a simple solution
citations.quote: Properly computed post-error slowing (correct-after-error minus correct-after-correct, matched on position) averages ~20–60 ms in speeded choice tasks.

citations.authors: Notebaert, W., Houtman, F., Van Opstal, F., Gevers, W., Fias, W., & Verguts, T.
citations.title: Post-error slowing: An orienting account
citations.quote: Across conflict tasks including Stroop, post-error slowing magnitudes typically fall in the 25–70 ms range when measured on the immediately following trial.

post_event_slowing.rationale: Post-error slowing of ~30-60 ms on the trial immediately following an error is a well-documented phenomenon in Stroop and other speeded conflict tasks, reflecting transient post-error caution.

citations.authors: Heathcote, A., Brown, S., & Mewhort, D. J. K.
citations.title: The power law repealed: The case for an exponential law of practice
citations.quote: Across choice-RT tasks, initial-block RT exceeds the asymptote by roughly 40–100 ms, with most of the gain absorbed by an exponential decay over the first several dozen trials.

citations.authors: Logan, G. D.
citations.title: Toward an instance theory of automatization
citations.quote: Speedup with practice on speeded-decision tasks shows an initial offset of tens to ~100 ms over asymptote, decaying with experience.

citations.authors: Heathcote, A., Brown, S., & Mewhort, D. J. K.
citations.title: The power law repealed: The case for an exponential law of practice
citations.quote: Exponential fits to practice curves indicate most of the speedup is captured within the first 1–3 blocks of ~50–100 trials.

citations.authors: MacLeod, C. M.
citations.title: Half a century of research on the Stroop effect: An integrative review
citations.quote: Stroop performance stabilizes after relatively brief practice (typically within the first one to two blocks), after which condition-specific effects dominate further change.

citations.authors: Spieler, D. H., Balota, D. A., & Faust, M. E.
citations.title: Stroop performance in healthy younger and older adults and in individuals with dementia of the Alzheimer's type
citations.quote: The color-word Stroop task was administered in blocks of approximately 48 trials.

citations.authors: Heathcote, A., Popiel, S. J., & Mewhort, D. J. K.
citations.title: Analysis of response time distributions: An example using the Stroop task
citations.quote: Stroop trials were administered in blocks of roughly 40–60 trials per condition.

citations.authors: Heathcote, A., Brown, S., & Mewhort, D. J. K.
citations.title: The power law repealed: The case for an exponential law of practice
citations.quote: Best-fit exponential decay rates for practice on choice-RT tasks span roughly 0.4–0.8 per block, with substantial individual variation.

citations.authors: Logan, G. D.
citations.title: Toward an instance theory of automatization
citations.quote: Practice-related speedup follows a power/exponential form whose decay implies the bulk of improvement is realized in the first few blocks.

practice_effect.rationale: Early blocks of Stroop show an RT decrease of ~50-80 ms relative to asymptote, with most of the improvement completed within the first 1-2 task blocks (~50-100 trials) as participants stabilize stimulus-response mappings.

vigilance_decrement.rationale: Vigilance decrement (increasing RT variability or omissions across blocks) is documented mainly for sustained-attention paradigms with long monotonic sessions; standard short Stroop blocks (a few hundred trials) do not reliably show this effect distinct from generic fatigue drift, so this mechanism is left disabled.

between_subject_jitter.rationale: Between-subject SD on mean Stroop RT in healthy adults is ~60-90 ms, with the Stroop effect itself (incongruent-congruent) showing SD of ~20-30 ms. Distribution shape parameters (sigma, tau) typically vary within ±15% across subjects. Accuracy is near ceiling with low between-subject SD (~0.02) and bounded by physiological response and rare lapses; omissions are rare (<5%) given the 1500 ms response window.

citations.authors: Hedge, C., Powell, G., & Sumner, P.
citations.title: The reliability paradox: Why robust cognitive tasks do not produce reliable individual differences
citations.quote: Between-subject SD on mean Stroop RT in healthy young adults was on the order of 60–90 ms across testing sessions.

citations.authors: Spieler, D. H., Balota, D. A., & Faust, M. E.
citations.title: Stroop performance in healthy younger and older adults and in individuals with dementia of the Alzheimer's type
citations.quote: Between-subject SD of mean Stroop RT in the young-adult sample was approximately 70–85 ms.

citations.authors: Hedge, C., Powell, G., & Sumner, P.
citations.title: The reliability paradox: Why robust cognitive tasks do not produce reliable individual differences
citations.quote: The Stroop interference effect (incongruent − congruent) showed a between-subject SD of approximately 20–30 ms across replications.

citations.authors: MacLeod, C. M.
citations.title: Half a century of research on the Stroop effect: An integrative review
citations.quote: Stroop interference magnitudes vary across individuals in the range of tens of milliseconds, typically 15–35 ms SD.

citations.authors: Heathcote, A., Popiel, S. J., & Mewhort, D. J. K.
citations.title: Analysis of response time distributions: An example using the Stroop task
citations.quote: Individual ex-Gaussian sigma and tau estimates varied across subjects on the order of ±15–20% relative to the group mean.

citations.authors: Spieler, D. H., Balota, D. A., & Faust, M. E.
citations.title: Stroop performance in healthy younger and older adults and in individuals with dementia of the Alzheimer's type
citations.quote: Sigma and tau showed proportional between-subject variation of roughly ±15% in young adults.

citations.authors: Hedge, C., Powell, G., & Sumner, P.
citations.title: The reliability paradox: Why robust cognitive tasks do not produce reliable individual differences
citations.quote: Stroop accuracy in healthy young adults is near ceiling, with between-subject SD on the order of 0.02–0.04.

citations.authors: MacLeod, C. M.
citations.title: Half a century of research on the Stroop effect: An integrative review
citations.quote: Error rates in color-word Stroop are typically below 10% with low between-subject variability.

citations.authors: Hedge, C., Powell, G., & Sumner, P.
citations.title: The reliability paradox: Why robust cognitive tasks do not produce reliable individual differences
citations.quote: Omission rates in standard Stroop sessions with response windows ≥1500 ms are typically <2%, with between-subject SD on the order of 0.005–0.01.

citations.authors: Hedge, C., Powell, G., & Sumner, P.
citations.title: The reliability paradox: Why robust cognitive tasks do not produce reliable individual differences
citations.quote: Per-subject accuracy in healthy young adults on Stroop spans roughly 0.85–0.99, with values below ~0.80 typically reflecting non-compliance and routinely excluded.

citations.authors: MacLeod, C. M.
citations.title: Half a century of research on the Stroop effect: An integrative review
citations.quote: Healthy adult accuracy on color-word Stroop is consistently >85% across published replications, with most participants between 90% and 99%.

citations.authors: Hedge, C., Powell, G., & Sumner, P.
citations.title: The reliability paradox: Why robust cognitive tasks do not produce reliable individual differences
citations.quote: Omission rates in standard Stroop are bounded below 5% in compliant young adult participants; values above this are typically grounds for exclusion.

citations.authors: Spieler, D. H., Balota, D. A., & Faust, M. E.
citations.title: Stroop performance in healthy younger and older adults and in individuals with dementia of the Alzheimer's type
citations.quote: In young-adult samples, omissions on color-word Stroop with a generous response window were rare, well below 5% across subjects.

reasoning_chain.inference: Identified paradigm 'stroop_rdoc' with 3 stimuli. Source files: jquery.min.js, bootstrap.min.js, math.min.js, jspsych.js, plugin-instructions.js, plugin-html-keyboard-response.js, plugin-fullscreen.js, plugin-survey-text.js, plugin-attention-check.js, experiment.j.
Produced ex-Gaussian (mu/sigma/tau) parameters for 2 conditions; enabled 7 temporal effects. Self-corrected after 1 schema-validation refinement(s).
Produced 56 citations across 26 numeric parameters with literature_range and between_subject_sd.
Tagged sensitivity on 32 parameters; 6 are high-sensitivity.
